nodes.task:
Convert the single-agent 'mutate' step in templates/workflows/daily-mutator.yaml into a parallel fan-out across the six role files. YAML-only change. No new tests; an existing parser test must stay green.

## Files to modify
- templates/workflows/daily-mutator.yaml — replace the existing `- id: mutate` step (currently `preset: cc-opus`, `worktree: true`, with a `task: |` body). Preserve `id: mutate`, `on_complete: eval`, `on_fail: abort`. Do NOT touch `collect`, `redact`, `find_skills`, `eval`, or `gate` steps.

## Required exact shape for the new step
```
  - id: mutate
    type: parallel
    n: 6
    presets: [cc-mutator, cc-mutator, cc-mutator, cc-mutator, cc-mutator, cc-mutator]
    step:
      id: mutate-child
      preset: cc-mutator
      worktree: true
      task: |
        You are a per-domain GEPA mutator. Map your $FLT_RUN_LABEL to one role file:
          a -> templates/roles/coder.md
          b -> templates/roles/reviewer.md
          c -> templates/roles/architect.md
          d -> templates/roles/oracle.md
          e -> templates/roles/mutator.md
          f -> templates/roles/evaluator.md
        If your label is anything else, flt workflow fail "unmapped label: $FLT_RUN_LABEL".
        Inputs:
        - $FLT_RUN_DIR/artifacts/trace-bundle-redacted.txt
        - $FLT_RUN_DIR/artifacts/skill-candidates.txt
        - Focus area: {task}
        Required outputs in $FLT_RUN_DIR/artifacts/ (label-scoped, must not collide):
        1) <role>-$FLT_RUN_LABEL.vNext.md with YAML front-matter (stable_path absolute, mutation_type: prompt_edit | skill_acquisition), then full proposed body.
        2) <role>-$FLT_RUN_LABEL.hypothesis.json shape: { artifact, mutation_type, hypothesis, expected_delta }.
        Rules: one candidate per child; stable_path absolute; do not edit the stable file in place.
        flt workflow pass when both files exist with label-scoped names.
        flt workflow fail "<reason>" only if you cannot mutate at all.
    on_complete: eval
    on_fail: abort
```

## Verification
- Run `bun test tests/unit/workflow-parser.test.ts` — every test (including the bundled-daily-mutator round-trip that asserts step ids `['collect','redact','find_skills','mutate','eval','gate']`) must pass.
- Inspect the file with `cat templates/workflows/daily-mutator.yaml` (or Read) to confirm indentation is consistent with the rest of the file (2-space).

## Notes
- The fixture in workflow-parser.test.ts already includes a `cc-mutator` preset, so no preset registry change is needed.
- Be careful with the `task: |` block scalar indentation. The rest of the file uses 6-space body indent under `task: |`; match it exactly so the YAML parses.
- Do NOT alter the `eval` parallel step or any other step.

Write a coder.md handoff file in your worktree summarising what you changed and the bun-test result.

flt workflow pass when tests are green and your coder.md handoff is written.

Make pending gates/questions visible in the TUI without requiring the user to press 'g'. Two changes: persistent badge in the status chrome, and a 5-second yellow banner on transitions where the count goes up.

## Files to modify
- src/tui/modal-gates.ts — add and export `getPendingGatesCount(): number`. It must return `scanGates().length + <qna group-count>`. The qna group-count is the number of distinct batches (each batchId counts once) plus singleton qna rows (rows with no batchId). Reuse the same logic that `loadAllRows()` (around line 68) uses to group by batchId — extract a helper if needed but DO NOT duplicate the grouping. `loadAllRows` already produces `[...gates, ...batchRows, ...singletons.map(qnaRowToModal)]`; the count must equal `gates.length + batchRows.length + singletons.length`. Easiest correct implementation: factor the qna grouping out of `loadAllRows` into a small private helper, call it from both `loadAllRows` and `getPendingGatesCount`.
- src/tui/panels.ts — render `(N gates)` (singular `(1 gate)`, otherwise `(N gates)`) near the existing chrome status indicators inside `renderStatusBar` (around line 641). Wire it in by calling the new exported helper from modal-gates.ts. Place the badge after the existing `summary` text (e.g. as a trailing segment) so it is always visible whenever the modal-gates state has any pending row. When count is 0, render nothing (no badge). Keep one-line and use the existing `putLine`/`screen.put` helpers and the existing theme colour conventions; nothing fancy.
- Banner-on-new-gate: in src/tui/modal-gates.ts inside `openGatesWatcher` (around line 162), the watcher callback already calls `loadAllRows` and `onChange`. Compare the new count against the previous count cached on the `GatesModalState` (add a numeric field, e.g. `lastNotifiedCount`, default 0). When the new count is strictly greater than `lastNotifiedCount`, call `app.setBanner('New gate: <run-id> <kind> — press g')` for 5 seconds and update `lastNotifiedCount` to the new value. When the count drops, just update `lastNotifiedCount` silently — no banner. The watcher must accept an `onBanner: (msg: string, ttlMs: number) => void` callback (passed in from app.ts wiring) so this module stays decoupled from the App singleton; if app.ts wiring is non-trivial, a thin `setBanner` callback parameter is enough — do NOT import the App directly here.
  - Wire the new callback through from src/tui/app.ts (search for `openGatesWatcher` call) and have it invoke whatever existing setBanner-equivalent the app already has. If `setBanner` doesn't exist yet, add a minimal yellow-banner field to AppState and render it for ttlMs in the next frame, then clear it; if that becomes more than ~30 LOC, leave the banner side as a TODO and ship just the badge — but do ship the badge.

## Tests (TDD: write first, watch fail, then implement)
- tests/unit/tui-gates-badge.test.ts (NEW). Pure formatter unit tests — no Screen, no app. Cover:
  - 0 → returns empty string `''` (no badge)
  - 1 → `'(1 gate)'`
  - 3 → `'(3 gates)'`
  Pull the formatter out as an exported pure function (e.g. `formatGatesBadge(count: number): string`) so it is unit-testable without a screen. Add it to either src/tui/modal-gates.ts or src/tui/panels.ts — wherever it's most natural. Skip the banner-trigger test if exercising the watcher requires the full app; add a one-line `// banner-trigger test skipped — requires full app wiring` comment in the test file.

## Verification
- `bun test tests/unit/tui-gates-badge.test.ts` must be green.
- Existing TUI tests (tests/unit/tui/*.test.ts) must still be green — run `bun test tests/unit/tui` to confirm.
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` must report no NEW errors compared to the pre-change baseline. (Run it before and after; record both counts in your handoff.)

## Constraints
- No `as any`, no `as unknown as` casts.
- No drive-by refactors. Touch only the files listed.
- Keep the public API of modal-gates.ts back-compatible — only add new exports, don't rename existing ones.

Write a coder.md handoff in your worktree describing the diff at file granularity and the test/typecheck output.

flt workflow pass when tests are green and your coder.md handoff is written.

Add a 'grill' step that asks the user clarifying questions BEFORE spec/architect runs in the two long-form workflows. The grill agent uses 'flt ask human' as a single batched call so the TUI shows them as one chained pick-flow.

## Files to modify
- templates/workflows/idea-to-pr.yaml — prepend a new `grill` step at the very top of `steps:` (before `spec`). Update the existing `spec` step's `task:` body so its FIRST line reads exactly: `Read {steps.grill.worktree}/grill-answers.md FIRST and treat the user's answers as binding.` Keep the existing spec body afterwards.
- templates/workflows/new-project.yaml — same change: prepend a `grill` step before the existing first step (currently `research`). Then update the existing `spec` step's `task:` body (which is currently a one-liner `'Using the research from inbox, write spec.md for: {task}.'`) so it begins with `Read {steps.grill.worktree}/grill-answers.md FIRST and treat the user's answers as binding. ` followed by the original instruction. The bootstrap names this a 'spec' step; new-project.yaml's first non-grill step IS `spec` (id: spec). Inspect the file before editing if uncertain.
- tests/unit/init-workflows.test.ts — this test currently only checks workflow names parse, not step ordering, so it likely needs no update. Read it first; if any assertion checks step-id arrays for these two workflows, update those expectations to include `grill` at the front. Do not loosen assertions — fix them precisely.

## Required exact shape for the grill step
```
  - id: grill
    preset: cc-sonnet
    task: |
      Original task: {task}
      Read the task above. Identify any genuine ambiguities or scope decisions a coder will need clarified to deliver an unambiguous implementation. Skip questions whose answer is obvious from the task. Cap at 3 questions. If the task is unambiguous, skip the call and write 'No clarifications needed.' to grill-answers.md.
      If you do have questions, build a single batch payload (Question objects in one array) and call:
        flt ask human '<json>'  # the json must contain a 'questions' array with all your questions in one shot — do NOT call ask human multiple times.
      Each Question must have: id (short kebab-case), header (one-line), question (full prompt), multiSelect (boolean), options (array of {label, description?}). Pre-author concrete options so the user picks rather than typing.
      After the call returns, write all Q&A to grill-answers.md in your worktree (one ## section per question with the user's chosen answer).
      flt workflow pass when grill-answers.md exists.
    on_complete: spec
```

This goes BEFORE the existing first step in each yaml. The existing first step's `on_complete` chain stays unchanged — `grill.on_complete: spec` simply hands off to it.

## Verification (TDD: read existing tests first, then edit)
- Run `bun test tests/unit/workflow-parser.test.ts tests/unit/init-workflows.test.ts` BEFORE making changes — record the current pass count.
- Make your edits.
- Run the same command — both files must be green. The workflow-parser test bundles a daily-mutator yaml round-trip and other validations; your changes shouldn't affect those, but verify they still pass.
- Also load both edited yaml files via `bun -e 'import { loadWorkflowDef } from "./src/workflow/parser"; console.log(loadWorkflowDef("idea-to-pr").steps.map(s => s.id))'` after seeding (or just write a one-liner test with `validateWorkflowDef(parse(readFileSync(...)))`) — the first id MUST be `grill`. Actually, the existing parser test already has examples of how to load a bundled workflow file via path; just adapt that approach to verify locally.

## Constraints
- YAML indentation must match the rest of each file (2-space). The `task: |` body indent in idea-to-pr.yaml is 6 spaces — match it.
- Do not touch any step besides the new `grill` step and the prepended line in the existing spec step.
- No drive-by edits to other workflows.

Write a coder.md handoff describing exactly which steps were added/modified per file and the bun-test output before/after.

flt workflow pass when tests are green and your coder.md handoff is written.
